topic: International Trade and the Global Economy
subject: economics

items.q: What does Income Elasticity of Demand (YED) tell us?
items.correct: The sign tells us the good type: positive YED = normal good (rises with income); negative YED = inferior good (falls with income). YED > 1 indicates a luxury; YED between 0 and 1 a necessity.
items.misconception: A negative YED means the demand calculation went wrong.
items.random_wrong: YED measures how the rich and poor differ in tastes.
items.why: The negative sign for inferior goods (like own-brand pasta or bus travel) is meaningful, not an error. Students often treat negative elasticities as mistakes, missing what the sign signals about good type.

items.q: What is asymmetric information as a source of market failure?
items.correct: When one party in a transaction knows more than the other — leading to problems like adverse selection (used-car market) or moral hazard (insurance). Markets can fail or unravel when information is unequal.
items.misconception: Asymmetric information means a buyer and seller disagree about a price.
items.random_wrong: Asymmetric information is when a market only operates in one direction.
items.why: Disagreement about value is normal trade. Asymmetric information is about one side genuinely knowing more — Akerlof's 'lemons' problem shows how this can destroy entire markets. Students collapse the concept into ordinary haggling.

items.q: Why do economists generally argue against protectionism?
items.correct: Tariffs and quotas raise prices for consumers, protect inefficient industries, invite retaliation, and reduce overall welfare — even when they save specific jobs, the cost per job saved typically exceeds the wage.
items.misconception: Protectionism always makes a country richer by keeping money at home.
items.random_wrong: Protectionism means protecting national parks from development.
items.why: Trade is positive-sum in standard theory — countries gain from specialisation. Students assume tariffs trap wealth domestically, missing that consumers pay higher prices and exporters lose from retaliation.

items.q: Specialisation based on lowest opportunity cost is:
items.why: Correct answer: Comparative advantage. “Absolute advantage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A tariff is a tax on:
items.why: It's Imports. “Exports” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did Ricardo publish his theory of comparative advantage?
items.why: The real figure is 1817 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
